label: Attention Schema Theory
definition: A theory of consciousness proposing that the brain constructs a simplified internal model of its own attention, an attention schema, and that subjective awareness is what that model reports; awareness is the brain's coarse self-description of the act of attending.

body.text: Attention schema theory holds that the brain builds a model of its own attention, just as it builds models of the body and of the outside world, and that consciousness is the content of that model. The brain controls attention, the process by which it selectively boosts some signals and suppresses others, and like any good controller it benefits from an internal representation of the thing it is controlling. That representation is the attention schema: a simplified, fast, not-quite-accurate sketch of what the system is currently attending to and what attending feels like to do. On this account, when a person reports being subjectively aware of something, they are reporting the contents of this schematic self-model, not directly perceiving any deeper inner light. Awareness is the brain's caricature of attention.

The theory was developed by the neuroscientist Michael Graziano and is deliberately mechanistic and deflationary about the puzzle of consciousness. Its central move is to explain not how subjective experience arises but why a brain would insist it has subjective experience. If the self-model of attention is coarse and omits the underlying neuronal machinery, then the system has no internal access to the physical details of its own processing and will describe attention in non-physical, ethereal terms, as a felt awareness rather than as electrochemical signaling. The very inaccuracy of the schema, in this view, is what produces the intuition that consciousness is something immaterial and hard to explain. The theory thereby reframes the hard problem as a predictable artifact of a useful but simplified self-representation.

Attention schema theory sits alongside, and partly inside, other functional accounts of mind. It is closely related to global-workspace-theory: where the global workspace describes the mechanism by which selected information is broadcast and made widely available, the attention schema describes the model the system has of that selection process, so the two can be read as complementary rather than competing. The theory leans on a control-system and predictive-coding logic, treating the schema as an internal model used for prediction and regulation in the same way a body schema is, and it connects awareness directly to attention, the same selective bottleneck that working-memory and the brain's processing limits impose. Graziano argues the schema also serves social cognition, since modeling attention in oneself and modeling it in others, attributing awareness to other minds, can use the same machinery.

For artificial intelligence the theory is unusually constructive, because it makes consciousness an engineering target rather than a mystery. If awareness just is a system's model of its own attention, then in principle one could give a machine an attention schema: a component that models, monitors, and reports on the system's own attention-mechanism, and the theory predicts such a system would, like us, claim to be aware and would use that self-model to control its processing more effectively. This makes attention schema theory one of the few accounts of consciousness with a clear implementation path, and it has been put forward both as a route toward machine awareness and as a way to ask, of a sufficiently self-modeling AI, whether it has the functional ingredients the theory says awareness consists of, separately from any metaphysical question about inner experience.
